summary: Apple broke with its long tradition of secrecy to pre-announce a completely redesigned modular Mac Pro, acknowledging that the 2013 cylindrical Mac Pro's tightly integrated thermal design was a mistake that couldn't accommodate the industry's shift toward large single GPUs. The announcement came at a small roundtable with five journalists at Apple's hardware prototyping lab, where Schiller, Federighi, and Ternus confirmed the new Mac Pro won't ship in 2017 but promised a modular, upgradeable design with Apple-branded pro displays. Apple also revealed that only 30% of Mac users qualify as 'pro' users, and among those, Mac Pro buyers represent a single-digit percentage of all Mac sales. Despite the small market, Apple framed this as a deep commitment to pro users, also teasing significant iMac updates targeting professionals. Gruber interprets the move as unprecedented but necessary — silence would have been untenable given growing fears that Apple was abandoning the pro market.
key_points: The 2013 Mac Pro's dual-GPU thermal design was a dead end — the industry moved to single large GPUs, and Apple couldn't adapt the enclosure
Apple chose to pre-announce future products over its deeply held tradition of secrecy because continued silence was untenable
Mac Pro users represent a single-digit percentage of Mac sales, yet Apple committed to building a new modular system for this small audience
Apple came as close to admitting a mistake as the company ever does, with Schiller saying 'we're sorry to disappoint customers'
Pro iMacs are positioned as the volume play for professionals, with the Mac Pro serving the extreme performance tier
money_quotes: I think we designed ourselves into a bit of a thermal corner, if you will.
The word 'mistake' was not uttered, but this is about as close as we're going to get to Apple admitting they miscalculated with the current Mac Pro's concept.
For Apple to care about the Mac Pro, it requires Apple to care about a small number of users.
If they had released these speed bumps without any comment about the future of the Mac Pro, people would have reasonably concluded that Apple had lost its goddamned mind.
Yes, those numbers are all peanuts compared to the iPhone, but everything is peanuts compared to the iPhone.
Ultimately, actions speak louder than words. But I very much like the words I heard yesterday.

One of the good things, hopefully, with Apple through the years has been a willingness to say when something isn't quite what we wanted it to be, didn't live up to expectations, to not be afraid to admit it and look for the next answer.
key_insight: Apple's unprecedented decision to pre-announce a future Mac Pro was an admission that the 2013 design was a strategic miscalculation, and that continued silence about the pro market had become more damaging than breaking the company's cardinal rule against pre-announcing products.
